current_fact.fact_text: The Cold War that followed 1945 wasn’t just a standoff of missiles and speeches—it was also a contest of stories, art, and music, from jazz tours used as soft power to rock broadcasts beamed over borders. On May 9, 1955, when West Germany joined NATO, it entered not only a military alliance but a cultural and ideological constellation.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: A global ideological standoff that spanned continents aligns with a multi-genre, border-crossing musical journey, reflecting how culture travels even when politics divide.

queue.fact_text: The Royal Navy, founded centuries earlier, played a central role in many of May 9’s wartime events—from escorting convoys to capturing U‑110. By World War II it was not only a fleet of steel and sailors, but a floating network of radar dishes, codebooks, and experimental technologies that turned the sea into a laboratory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A storied naval force and its ships at sea fit naturally with an episode literally recorded on a boat, celebrating life, stories, and music on the water.

queue.fact_text: The Food and Drug Administration, which approved the first birth control pill on May 9, 1960, has roots in early 20th‑century efforts to keep dangerous chemicals and fake medicines off shelves. Over time, this quiet bureaucracy gained the power to decide which scientific breakthroughs would safely reach everyday people—and how fast.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: An agency built on testing, evidence, and gradual progress toward safer medicines fits an introspective episode about science, learning curves, and slow, cumulative change.

published.fact_text: In 1926, American aviator Richard E. Byrd reported that he and pilot Floyd Bennett had flown over the North Pole in a Fokker trimotor—an announcement that electrified the world with visions of conquering Earth’s most remote point. Decades later, Byrd’s diary suggested their flight may have turned back early, leaving historians with a fascinating mix of daring exploration and enduring mystery.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: An Arctic exploration story pairs naturally with an episode about journeys beyond the polar circle and the meditative awe of icy oceans.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 1941, the German submarine U-110 was captured intact by the Royal Navy in the North Atlantic, yielding an Enigma machine and codebooks. This secret haul became a turning point in the Allied codebreaking effort, allowing cryptanalysts at Bletchley Park to read vast streams of German naval communications.

Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

published.match_reason: Breaking Enigma was an immense intellectual challenge, echoing the episode’s focus on persistence, problem‑solving, and the deep satisfaction of hard-won understanding.

published.fact_text: In 1502, Christopher Columbus departed from Cádiz on his fourth and final voyage, not in search of a new continent but of a western passage to Asia. Instead, he was battered by storms, marooned in Jamaica, and pushed to the edges of his reputation, turning this journey into a story of stubborn hope colliding with harsh reality.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: Columbus’s sea voyage from Spain mirrors an episode built around travel, landscapes, and sailors’ songs, especially the track dedicated to seafarers.

published.fact_text: Hernando de Alarcón set sail in 1540 on a little-remembered expedition up the Gulf of California, becoming one of the first Europeans to navigate the lower Colorado River. While others scoured for mythical golden cities, Alarcón carefully mapped currents and coastlines, leaving behind a quietly revolutionary chart of an unfamiliar sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A river-and-gulf exploration fits a water-themed episode about immersion, currents, and the slow mapping of unknown depths.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 1960, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved Enovid as the world’s first oral contraceptive pill, transforming it from a hormone treatment into a tool for birth control. This decision quietly shifted power over reproduction toward those who could take a small tablet every day, reshaping relationships, careers, and social norms across the globe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: The pill’s approval is a landmark in women’s autonomy, resonating with an episode celebrating powerful female voices and self-determination.

published.fact_text: In 1980, the Liberian freighter MV Summit Venture struck a support column of Florida’s Sunshine Skyway Bridge during a sudden squall, sending a 430-metre section of roadway collapsing into Tampa Bay. The tragedy killed 35 people and forced engineers to rethink how bridges could be built to withstand both ships and storms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A disaster triggered by a sudden storm connects with an episode themed around rain, resilience, and finding hope after violent weather—literal and emotional.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 2001, a football match in Accra, Ghana, turned catastrophic when police fired tear gas into the stands after fans protested a referee’s call. The resulting panic and stampede at the Accra Sports Stadium left 129 people dead, leading to sweeping reforms in stadium safety and crowd control across Africa.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A major event in Ghanaian football history pairs with an episode that opens with Ghanaian highlife, reflecting on how music and public life intertwine on the continent.

published.fact_text: In 1920, during the Polish–Soviet War, Polish forces under General Edward Śmigły-Rydz marched into Kyiv and staged a victory parade along Khreshchatyk, the city’s main avenue. The moment was triumphant but fleeting: within weeks, Soviet counterattacks forced a retreat, turning the parade into a poignant snapshot of a contested city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A key episode in Polish history connects with an episode built around a modern journey through Poland, weaving past and present landscapes together.

published.fact_text: The Exhibition of 1761, held by the Society of Artists of Great Britain in London’s Spring Gardens, was one of the first large-scale public art shows in the city. For a small fee, visitors could wander rooms filled with paintings and sculptures, an early hint that art would gradually move from aristocratic halls into public life.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: An inaugural public art exhibition mirrors the first Sedna FM episode—both are bold openings that invite a broader audience into a creative space.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 1945, the Channel Islands—Jersey, Guernsey, and their neighbors—were liberated from nearly five years of Nazi occupation, a unique corner of British territory under German rule. When British ships finally appeared on the horizon, islanders rushed to the harbors waving homemade flags, turning the shoreline into a spontaneous festival of relief.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: The liberation of small sea-bound islands fits the episode’s coastal, sunlit mood—moving from occupation and scarcity into an almost endless-feeling summer of freedom.

published.fact_text: England and Portugal formalized the Treaty of Windsor in 1386, cementing the Anglo-Portuguese Alliance—often cited as the oldest continuously operating diplomatic alliance in the world. Born in a medieval world of dynastic marriages and seafaring ambitions, it has quietly outlived empires, revolutions, and shifting borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: An ocean-crossing alliance between two maritime powers resonates with a seafaring, Mediterranean-themed musical voyage across coasts and cultures.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 1974, the U.S. House Judiciary Committee opened public impeachment hearings against President Richard Nixon, bringing the Watergate scandal into millions of living rooms. For weeks, viewers watched a slow, forensic unravelling of political power, as tapes, memos, and testimonies reframed what leadership and accountability should look like.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A national reckoning broadcast through television lines echoes an episode about media, technology, and how our channels of connection reshape public and private life.

published.fact_text: West Germany joined NATO on May 9, 1955, just a decade after World War II, signaling its reintegration into a Western security alliance. The move was controversial, but it marked a pivot from defeated enemy to essential partner in a new, tense balance between East and West.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

published.match_reason: NATO’s story is rooted in postwar Western Europe and the UK’s diplomatic orbit, tying thematically to an episode steeped in London’s modern, globally connected energy.

published.fact_text: In 1918, British forces attempted a second time to block the German-held port of Ostend in Belgium by scuttling old warships in its channel. The plan was daring but only partly successful, illustrating how even in the final months of World War I, navies were still improvising with bold, almost cinematic raids.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A high-risk naval raid with explosions and night maneuvers fits the raw, electric, high-adrenaline rock energy of this episode.

published.fact_text: The crash of the Vienna Stock Exchange on May 9, 1873—known as "Der Krach"—triggered a cascade of bank failures that spiraled into the Long Depression. What began as speculative overconfidence in railways and real estate exposed how fragile the 19th‑century global financial system had quietly become.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: A spectacular financial collapse born from the illusion of perfection fits an episode about embracing flaws, limits, and the beauty of things not going to plan.

published.fact_text: The mischievous figure later known as Mr. Punch made his first recorded appearance in England in 1662, in a performance noted in Samuel Pepys’s diary. Over time, this cackling puppet evolved into the star of "Punch and Judy" shows, turning slapstick chaos into a strangely enduring mirror of social misrule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: A rowdy, crowd-pleasing puppet show that delighted generations pairs with a playful, nostalgic episode celebrating guilty pleasures and light-hearted fun.

published.fact_text: In 1310, Nephon I, formerly Bishop of Cyzicus, was consecrated as Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople, taking on the role of first among equals in Eastern Orthodox leadership. His tenure unfolded in a city where theology, politics, and imperial intrigue were tightly knotted together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A spiritual leader’s consecration in a city of shifting powers connects with an episode about transition, grounding rituals, and the places that anchor us when everything is in motion.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 2022, U.S. President Joe Biden signed the Ukraine Democracy Defense Lend-Lease Act, reviving a World War II–era mechanism to speed weapons and aid to Ukraine and other Eastern European allies. The act symbolically bridged two eras of conflict, suggesting that old tools can be reactivated in new geopolitical storms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: Reactivating a decades-old wartime policy in a modern conflict fits an episode about rethinking assumptions, revisiting old ideas, and seeing them with new eyes.

published.fact_text: LOT Polish Airlines Flight 5055 crashed near Warsaw in 1987 after an engine disintegrated in flight, severing control systems and causing a catastrophic fire. The crew fought to keep the damaged Ilyushin Il‑62 airborne long enough to turn back, leaving behind radio recordings that still move investigators and listeners decades later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A devastating crash and the human effort inside the cockpit connect with an episode about bodily vulnerability, trauma, and the long, reflective process of healing afterwards.

published.fact_text: In Norco, California, in 1980, five heavily armed men robbed a bank and triggered a running gun battle that became one of the largest police pursuits in the state’s history. The shootout, involving high-powered weapons and even helicopters, exposed how unprepared local law enforcement was for militarized crime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A violent confrontation, heavy weapons, and systemic questions about policing align with the episode’s intense, politically charged rock about power, violence, and resistance.

published.fact_text: On May 9, 1941, the same year U‑110 was captured, the broader context of World War II was one of encrypted messages, radar experiments, and rapidly evolving technology. The war became a catalyst for advances in computing, communications, and operational research that quietly laid foundations for the digital world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: World War II’s hidden technological revolution parallels the story of Auto‑Tune—an invisible tool that quietly reshaped how systems and sounds work behind the scenes.
